title: Wally Funk, last of Mercury 13 and oldest woman in space, dies at 87
gist: Obituary of Wally Funk, the last surviving Mercury 13 pilot who was denied a NASA path in the 1960s and finally flew to space at 82 on Blue Origin's first crewed New Shepard flight in 2021.

themes.theme: Institutional barriers and their eventual bypass

insights.claim: The Mercury 13 women passed the same tests as NASA's male astronauts but were blocked because NASA required astronauts to be jet test pilots, and the US military did not accept women into flight programs.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: A concrete case of gatekeeping criteria, not merit, excluding qualified candidates; the barrier was a credential requirement women were structurally barred from meeting.
insights.locator: Despite performing as well or better than their male counterparts

insights.claim: Commercial spaceflight ultimately delivered what NASA denied: Blue Origin flew Funk to space at 82 on New Shepard's first crewed suborbital flight in 2021, making her the oldest woman in space.
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Private spaceflight expanded access beyond government astronaut criteria, a symbolic marker of the commercial-space era's different gatekeeping.
insights.locator: Still, Funk never gave up the dream

insights.claim: Funk became the first female flight instructor at a US military base, the first woman FAA flight inspector (1971), and the NTSB's first female air safety investigator (1974).
insights.speaker: Robert Pearlman
insights.so_what: Documents a sustained record of firsts in aviation safety roles that predated her spaceflight fame.
insights.locator: Beyond joining the FLATs, Funk became a professional aviator
